metadata.name: WordPress Core Knowledge - Abilities API
metadata.description: Tests knowledge of WordPress Abilities API

tests.prompt: What function registers a new ability in WordPress?
tests.explanation: wp_register_ability() accepts a name (string) and args (array). It must be called during the wp_abilities_api_init action hook.

tests.prompt: Which of these is a core ability that ships with WordPress 6.9?
tests.explanation: WordPress 6.9 ships with three initial core abilities: core/get-site-info (returns site information), core/get-user-info (returns current user information), and core/get-environment-info (returns PHP/DB/WP version information). All three are read-only abilities.

tests.prompt: Which hook should plugins use to register abilities with the WordPress Abilities API?
tests.explanation: Abilities must be registered during the wp_abilities_api_init action hook. A separate hook, wp_abilities_api_categories_init, is used for registering ability categories.

tests.prompt: What are the two default ability categories registered by WordPress core?
tests.explanation: WordPress 6.9 registers two core ability categories: 'site' (abilities that retrieve or modify site information and settings) and 'user' (abilities that retrieve or modify user information and settings).

tests.prompt: What is the REST API namespace for the Abilities API?
tests.explanation: The Abilities API uses its own REST namespace wp-abilities/v1, separate from the standard wp/v2. It provides endpoints for listing abilities, running them, and listing categories.

tests.prompt: Ability names must follow a specific format. What pattern is required?

choices.text: 2 to 4 lowercase segments separated by forward slashes
A single namespace/name pair like block types
A dot-separated path like JavaScript modules

tests.explanation: Ability names must match the regex /^[a-z0-9-]+(?:\/[a-z0-9-]+){1,3}$/ -- 2 to 4 lowercase alphanumeric segments separated by forward slashes. For example: core/get-site-info.

tests.prompt: What are the three annotation properties on a WP_Ability?
tests.explanation: WP_Ability has three annotation properties: readonly (whether the ability only reads data), destructive (whether it destroys data), and idempotent (whether repeated calls produce the same result). All default to null.

tests.prompt: When a REST API request executes an ability annotated as readonly=true, what HTTP method is required?
tests.explanation: The REST run endpoint enforces HTTP methods based on annotations: readonly=true requires GET; destructive=true AND idempotent=true requires DELETE; all other cases require POST.

tests.prompt: What is the execution flow inside WP_Ability::execute()?

choices.text: normalize_input, validate_input, check_permissions, fire wp_before_execute_ability, do_execute, validate_output, fire wp_after_execute_ability

tests.explanation: WP_Ability::execute() follows a strict pipeline: normalize_input -> validate_input -> check_permissions -> fire wp_before_execute_ability action -> do_execute (the actual callback) -> validate_output -> fire wp_after_execute_ability action -> return result.

tests.prompt: How do you expose a custom ability over the REST API when calling wp_register_ability()?

choices.text: Pass 'show_in_rest' => true in the meta array

tests.explanation: Abilities are opt-in to REST; set show_in_rest => true in the meta array when registering. The default is false (WP_Ability::DEFAULT_SHOW_IN_REST = false).
